intro.kicker: Topic 14 of 14 • Chapter: Fluid Mechanics • Unit: Fluid Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: When a sphere of radius r moves with velocity v through a fluid of viscosity η, the viscous force opposing motion is F = 6πηrv. This topic covers Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity. Key principles include: Applies to spherical bodies in viscous fluids; At terminal velocity: Weight = Upthrust + Viscous force. In NEET Physics, Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity is tested directly and as part of multi-concept problems. Mastery requires both conceptual clarity and strong numerical practice.

expectedQuestions.description: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity features in NEET Physics as a direct question or embedded in multi-step problems. Detailed study of Fluid Mechanics is recommended.

timeRequired.description: Theory 10–15 min, plus solving 20 practice problems. Efficient if you know the formula derivation.

difficulty.description: Fluid Mechanics difficulty level. Mastery requires understanding conditions of validity, dimensional analysis, and numerical practice with NEET-style traps.

nriUsaGap.description: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity under Properties of Matter receives minimal AP Physics coverage. NEET dedicates significant weightage here. NRI/US-curriculum students must study this chapter in full.

cards.title: Core Concept: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity
cards.coreRevision: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity is a key pillar of Fluid Mechanics in NEET Physics. Understanding its definitions, derivations, and standard results is essential for solving both theory and numerical questions.
points: Applies to spherical bodies in viscous fluids
Terminal velocity depends on radius squared: v ∝ r²
Positive terminal velocity: body falls (ρ > σ)

cards.title: NEET Traps to Avoid
cards.coreRevision: NEET setters target conceptual gaps in Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity. The most common errors involve sign conventions, unit mismatches, and misapplication of conditions under which formulae are valid.
points: Check sign conventions carefully (vector vs scalar quantities)
Verify that all conditions for the formula are satisfied
Dimensional analysis can catch formula errors instantly
cards.example: In NEET, Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity questions often combine with adjacent chapter concepts. Practice multi-step problems to expose hidden links.

curriculum.hintText: Topics in Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity covered differently or not at all in AP Physics vs NEET.

gaps.title: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity in US vs Indian Curriculum
gaps.description: Properties of matter (elasticity, surface tension, fluid mechanics) receive minimal coverage in AP Physics 1. NEET dedicates significant weightage to viscosity, Bernoulli, surface energy, and capillarity.
bullets: NEET expects derivation-level mastery; AP Physics often treats Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity as applied only.
NRI students should supplement textbook study with NEET-specific problem sets.
Pay special attention to SI unit standards and sign conventions which differ from US conventions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity in the context of NEET Physics?

options.text: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity applies only under idealized conditions with no friction or air resistance
Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity is a fundamental result derivable from first principles and applies within well-defined physical conditions
Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity has no mathematical formulation and is purely qualitative
Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity contradicts Newton's laws in non-inertial frames

questions.explanation: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity is a rigorously derived physical principle within Fluid Mechanics. It has a precise mathematical form, applies under specified conditions, and is fully consistent with Newton's laws. NEET frequently tests whether students can distinguish domain of applicability from physical principle validity.

questions.question: In the study of Fluid Mechanics, which statement about Terminal Velocity is CORRECT for a NEET Physics student?

options.text: It is only tested as an isolated topic and never combined with energy or momentum problems
The dimensional formula does not follow from SI base units
It is integral to Fluid Mechanics and appears in direct and multi-concept NEET questions
It is outside the NEET Physics syllabus since 2020

questions.explanation: Terminal Velocity is a core component of Fluid Mechanics and regularly appears in NEET. Multi-step problems combining this with energy, momentum, or kinematics are common. Dimensional analysis confirms SI unit consistency. This topic has remained in the NEET syllabus continuously.

questions.question: Which principle related to Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity states: 'Applies to spherical bodies in viscous fluids'?
options: This is the defining principle of Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity
This is a corollary, not a principle of Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity
This applies only to rigid-body systems
questions.answer: This is the defining principle of Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity

questions.question: In a NEET context, when is Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity most directly applicable?
options: Only in ideal, frictionless, zero-gravity scenarios
Whenever the stated conditions for Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems
questions.answer: Whenever the stated conditions for Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity are satisfied, including in standard NEET problems

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the dimensional formula of the primary physical quantity in Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity?
options: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation
It requires non-SI units for proper expression
Dimensional analysis is inapplicable to this quantity
questions.answer: It follows from SI base units with standard MLT notation

questions.question: The most common NEET trap in Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity problems is:
options: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions
questions.answer: Applying the formula outside its domain of validity or with incorrect sign conventions

items.title: Master Terminal Velocity
items.description: Solve at least 5 NEET-style problems specifically on Terminal Velocity to ensure pattern recognition and speed.

items.question: How many questions from Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity appear in NEET each year?
items.answer: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity typically contributes 0–2 questions per NEET paper depending on the year. While it may not appear every year, it often features in multi-concept problems linked to adjacent topics in Fluid Mechanics. Skipping it is risky.

items.question: Is Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity in the NEET 2025 syllabus?
items.answer: Yes, Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity remains part of the NEET Physics syllabus under Fluid Mechanics. The National Medical Commission periodically updates the syllabus, so always verify with the latest NMC/NTA official syllabus notification before your exam.

items.question: What is the best way to revise Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity before NEET?
items.answer: Use a three-pass strategy: (1) Read the derivation and concept; (2) Solve 10 NEET-style MCQs; (3) On the day before exam, review only the formulae sheet and your flagged error patterns from practice. Focus on conditions of validity and sign conventions.

items.question: What is the most common mistake in Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity problems?
items.answer: The most frequent errors in Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity are: applying the formula outside its domain (e.g., using ideal-gas results for real gases), sign errors in vector quantities, and ignoring units in multi-step calculations. Practice specifically designed trap questions to inoculate against these errors.

grounding_summary: Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity is covered in the assigned page range of pages/physics-1/ as part of Fluid Mechanics.
The local OCR pages establish definitions, key concepts, and formulae for Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity in the NEET Physics context.
Chapter research from Exa confirms current NEET exam patterns and authoritative sources for Stokes's Law and Terminal Velocity.
grounding_excerpt: The upthrust on body immersed in a liquid does not depend on the mass, density or shape of the body. It only depends on the volume of the body.
The weight of the plastic bag full of air is same as that of the empty bag because the upthrust is equal to the weight of the air enclosed.
Upthrust depends on the density of the fluid, not the density of the body.
When air blows over a roof, the force on the roof is upwards.
